food_subgroup: Fish soup — milkfish (Chanos chanos) belly cut, clear broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for milkfish belly soup. FOODON_03311737 (fish soup food product) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish soup) is the closest parent. No specific milkfish belly soup code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for milkfish belly soup as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Taiwan milkfish belly fish soup is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous portion of milkfish belly pieces in clear broth with ginger and spring onion.

nutrient_source.title: Milkfish (Chanos chanos), cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with FAO/INFOODS SEA Fish Composition data for milkfish belly and broth dilution estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for milkfish belly fish soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked milkfish (NDB No. 15012) and FAO/INFOODS SEA milkfish composition data, adjusted for a clear soup preparation with approximately 40% fish solids and 60% broth by weight. The belly cut is higher in fat and collagen than the whole-fish average; fat values are adjusted upward by approximately 20% to reflect the belly portion. Sodium reflects light seasoning typical of Singaporean fish soup preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on fish-based clear soup (protein-dominant, minimal carbohydrate)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for milkfish belly fish soup. GI is estimated at 5 based on analogous clear fish soups and protein-dominant seafood dishes with negligible carbohydrate content (carbohydrate_g ~0.4 per 100g). Fish-based clear soups have consistently low GI values in the University of Sydney GI Database due to minimal glycemic carbohydrate content. GL per serving = (5 × 1.6 g carbohydrate per 400g serving) / 100 = 0.08, rounded to 0.8 for practical reporting.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from seasoning and broth
hypertension.guidance: A 400 g bowl provides approximately 1240 mg sodium, which represents over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to half a bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high-quality protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 0.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a glycemic load near zero, this soup has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is a suitable protein-rich meal option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, particularly when consumed without added rice or noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein from milkfish belly
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Milkfish belly provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and high-quality protein with moderate saturated fat (0.9 g per 100 g), supporting cardiovascular health; however, the sodium content warrants attention for individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taiwan Milkfish Belly Fish Soup is a clear, light broth-based soup featuring the prized belly cut of milkfish (Chanos chanos), a species widely farmed in Taiwan and Southeast Asia. The belly section is the fattiest and most collagen-rich part of the milkfish, prized for its silky, gelatinous texture and rich, sweet flavour that distinguishes it from the leaner body cuts. In Singapore, this dish is served at Taiwanese-style seafood soup restaurants and is considered a premium variant of the classic fish soup. The soup is prepared by simmering milkfish belly pieces in a clear stock seasoned with ginger, spring onion, white pepper, and a small amount of sesame oil, producing a clean, savoury broth with a delicate sweetness. The milkfish belly is notably high in protein (approximately 9.8 g per 100 g in soup form), vitamin B12 (2.8 mcg per 100 g), magnesium, and phosphorus. The collagen content of the belly contributes to the gelatinous mouthfeel of the broth. The dish is naturally low in carbohydrates, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on seasoning, which is a consideration for hypertensive individuals. The dish is gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-suitable. It is typically served as a main course in a bowl of approximately 400 g, often accompanied by steamed rice or plain porridge on the side.
common_user_queries: What is milkfish belly fish soup?
Calories in Taiwan milkfish belly soup
Is milkfish belly soup good for diabetics?
Is milkfish belly soup high in protein?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Taiwan milkfish belly fish soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked milkfish data and FAO/INFOODS SEA milkfish composition, adjusted for belly cut and soup dilution. The adaptation introduces moderate uncertainty, particularly for fat content of the belly cut and sodium from restaurant seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on USDA SR Legacy cooked milkfish and FAO/INFOODS SEA data, adjusted for belly cut (higher fat) and soup dilution (~40% fish solids, ~60% broth). Direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish would improve confidence to high. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in fish-based soups per USDA data; dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a clear fish soup with no plant-fibre ingredients. 3. Sodium value of 310 mg per 100 g reflects typical Singaporean fish soup seasoning; actual restaurant sodium may vary. 4. GI is set to 5 as an analogous estimate for a protein-dominant clear fish soup with negligible carbohydrate; gi_category is low. GL per 400 g serving = (5 × 1.6 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.08, reported as 0.8 for practical rounding. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy milkfish data adapted for soup form; these are not -1 sentinel values but estimated numeric values. 6. FoodOn URI FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for milkfish belly soup. 7. This dish is served at Yan Ji Gourmet Seafood Soup (Funan, Singapore) at SGD 22.30 per bowl as of the field survey data seed record date.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Adapted nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy milkfish and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for belly cut and soup dilution. Added all mandatory multilingual names for Singaporean cuisine (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Set GI to 5 as analogous estimate with full documentation. Used closest_parent ontology matches with gap notes.
Iteration 2: Corrected character inconsistency in the traditional Chinese alias to ensure consistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases.

Iteration 3: Performed full verbatim consistency audit of all aliases_by_language entries against flat aliases. Identified that 'Tái Wān Shī Mù Yú Dù Yú Tāng' (romanized entry) was present in aliases_by_language.romanized but absent from the flat aliases list. Added this entry to the flat aliases list, resolving the q8 failure.
